The most recent amendment is the 106th CA which introduced a reservation of one-third of seats for women in the Lok Sabha, State LAs and the Delhi LA for a period of 15 years from its commencement.

subject: Polity
question_text:
As per Article 368 of the Constitution of India, the Parliament may amend any provision of the Constitution by way of:
1. Addition
2. Variation
3. Repeal
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [IAS 2024]
solution:
(D) 1, 2 and 3
As per Article 368(2), the Parliament may amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of the Constitution.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following statements about the Estimates Committee of the Parliament is not correct? [CBI 2023]

options.a: The term of office of the Committee is five years.
options.d: The Committee suggests the form in which estimates shall be presented to the Parliament.

solution:
(A) The term of office of the Committee is five years.
The Estimates Committee is a financial standing committee which is reconstituted every year. Thus, its members have a term of 1 year only. Its function is to examine estimates included in the budget.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements about a State under the President's rule is/are correct?
1. The budget of a State under the President's rule is approved by the Governor of the State.
2. The procedure to pass the budget of a State under the President's rule is same as that of the budget of the Union Government.
Select the correct answer using the code given below. [CBI 2023]
solution:
(B) 2 only
Under President's Rule (Article 356), the State Legislature is either suspended or dissolved, and the Parliament (not the Governor) approves the State's budget. The Governor only acts as a constitutional head; he does not approve the budget. The budget is passed by Parliament, following the same procedure as the Union Budget.

subject: Polity
question_text: Who appoints the Chairperson of the Public Accounts Committee of the Parliament? [CISF AC 2022]

options.d: Leader of the majority party in the Lok Sabha

solution:
(A) Speaker, Lok Sabha
For explanation, refer to answer 52.

subject: Polity
question_text: In which one of the following cases did the Supreme Court of India adjudge that the proclamation of the President's Rule under Article 356 of the Constitution of India is subject to judicial review? [CISF AC 2022]

options.a: A.K. Gopalan vs. State of Madras
options.b: S.R. Bommai and Others vs. Union of India
options.c: Madhav Rao vs. Union of India
options.d: S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India

solution:
(B) S.R. Bommai vs. Union of India
In the S.R. Bommai vs. Union of India (1994), the Supreme Court ruled that the proclamation of President's Rule under Article 356 is subject to judicial review and emphasized that the President's satisfaction can be challenged in court if found to be mala fide or based on irrelevant grounds. A.K. Gopalan vs. State of Madras - Related to personal liberty and preventive detention. Madhav Rao vs. Union of India - Concerned with the abolition of privy purses. S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India - Dealt with judicial appointments and independence of the judiciary.

subject: Polity
question_text:
With respect to the Public Accounts Committee of the Parliament of India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. The committee came into existence in the year 1921.
II. It was not a Parliamentary Committee under the control of the Speaker at the time of its inception.
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [CISF AC 2023]
solution:
(C) Both I and II
The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) was first established in 1921 during British rule, following the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms, 1919. At the time of its inception, the PAC was not a Parliamentary Committee under the control of the Speaker, as India did not have a fully sovereign parliamentary system under British rule. It later became a parliamentary committee.

subject: Polity
question_text: What is the quorum for a meeting of the Committee on Petitions of the Rajya Sabha? [CISF AC 2024]
solution:
(B) Five
The quorum for a meeting of the Committee on Petitions of the Rajya Sabha is five members. It consists of 10 members nominated by the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. The Committee on Petitions examines petitions from the public concerning bills, policies, and issues of general interest, facilitating public participation in legislative processes.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following is/are exclusive power/powers of the Rajya Sabha?
1. The power to initiate a Money Bill
2. To declare a subject in the State List as a subject of national importance
3. To impeach the President of India
Select the correct answer using the code given below. [CISF AC 2024]
solution:
(B) 2 only
Exclusive powers of the Rajya Sabha: (1) Power under Article 249 - Legislation on State List in National Interest. Rajya Sabha can pass a resolution by a special majority declaring that it is necessary in the national interest for Parliament to make laws on a matter in the State List. The Lok Sabha has no corresponding power in this regard. (2) Power under Article 312 - Creation of All-India Services. Rajya Sabha can pass a resolution by special majority to authorize the Parliament to create new All-India Services.

subject: Polity
question_text: In India, on which one of the following grounds can the salary and allowances of persons serving in connection with affairs of the State be reduced? [CISF AC 2024]

options.c: Failure of the constitutional machinery in a State

solution:
(A) Financial Emergency
Under Article 360, during a Financial Emergency, the President can direct the reduction of salaries and allowances of those serving in connection with State affairs.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following statements:
1. A bill amending the Constitution requires a prior recommendation of the President of India.
2. When a Constitution Amendment Bill is presented to the President of India, it is obligatory for the President of India to give his/her assent.
3. A Constitution Amendment Bill must be passed by both the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha by a special majority and there is no provision for joint sitting.
Which of the statements given above are correct? [IAS 2022]
solution:
(B) 2 and 3
For explanation, refer to question 46 of level II.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following statements:
I. According to the Constitution of India, the Central Government has a duty to protect States from internal disturbances.
II. The Constitution of India exempts the States from providing legal counsel to a person being held for preventive detention.
III. According to the Prevention of Terrorism Act, 2002, confession of the accused before the police cannot be used as evidence.
How many of the above statements are correct? [IAS 2023]
solution:
(B) Only two
Article 355 states that the Union shall protect every State against external aggression and internal disturbance. Hence, statement 1 is correct. Article 22(1) provides the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of one's choice. However, under preventive detention [Article 22(5)], some rights are limited - but not the right to legal counsel completely. Hence, statement 2 is correct. Under POTA, 2002, confessions made to police officers of the rank of SP or above were admissible as evidence in court. Hence, statement 3 is incorrect.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Indian Constitution mentions that there shall be a Supreme Court of India? [SSC PHASE XII 24/06/2024]
solution:
(D) Article 124
Article 124 to 147 of Part V deals with the provisions of the Supreme Court. Article 124 - Establishment of the Supreme Court. (1) Strength of Judges - Chief Justice + number of judges are decided by the Parliament. (2) Appointment - The President appoints the CJI and other judges. While appointing, the President takes the recommendation of the CJI and the collegium system. (3) Qualification - A person must be a citizen of India or have been a judge of a High Court for at least 5 years or have been an advocate of a High Court for at least 10 years or be a distinguished jurist in the opinion of the President. (4) Tenure - holds office until the age of 65 years (no minimum age) and can resign by writing to the President. (5) Removal of a judge: on grounds of proved misbehavior or incapacity.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following Article of the Indian Constitution mentions the Supreme Court as Court of record? [SSC GD 13/02/2024]
solution:
(A) Article 129
Article 129 - Supreme Court to be a court of record and can punish for contempt of itself. Article 124 - There shall be a Supreme Court. Article 125 - Salaries of judges. Article 126 - Acting Judges. Article 127 - Ad hoc Judges. Article 128 - Retired judges. Article 130 - Delhi as the seat of the Supreme Court. Article 131 - Original jurisdiction of the SC.

subject: Polity
question_text: Under which Schedule of the Indian Constitution is the form of oath or affirmation for a Judge of the Supreme Court mentioned? [SSC PHASE XII 20/06/2024]
solution:
(C) Third Schedule
The Third Schedule contains the forms of oaths or affirmations of the Judges of the Supreme Court. Article 124 (6) - Every Judge of the Supreme Court takes oath before the President.

subject: Polity
question_text: In India the Salaries of Supreme Court Judges are determined by _______. [SSC GD 24/02/2024]
solution:
(B) Parliament of India
According to Article 125(1) of the Indian Constitution, the salaries, allowances and privileges of Supreme Court judges are determined by the Parliament by law. The salaries and other conditions of service shall not be varied to their disadvantage after appointment.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Constitution of India mentions about enlargement of the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court? [SSC JE 07/06/2024]
solution:
(A) Article 138
Article 138 - enlargement of the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. It allows the Parliament to extend the jurisdiction and powers of the Supreme Court with respect to matters in the Union or State list. Article 140 - Parliament may confer additional powers on the Supreme Court to enable it to perform its functions more effectively. Article 142 - Enforcement of decrees and orders of the Supreme Court for doing complete justice in any matter before it. Article 144 - All authorities in India (civil and judicial) must act in aid of the Supreme Court.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Indian judicial system is based on: [SSC CPO 11/11/2022]
solution:
(D) Single integrated system
There is one unified judiciary for the entire country. The Supreme Court is at the top, followed by High Courts and then Subordinate Courts. Article 144 - Civil and judicial authorities to act in aid of the Supreme Court.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Supreme Court of India came into existence on _______. [SSC CGL 24/07/2023]
solution:
(B) 26 January 1950
The Supreme Court of India came into existence on 26th January, 1950, the same day the Constitution of India came into force, replacing the Federal Court of India established under the Government of India Act, 1935. Its inaugural session was held on 28 January, 1950 with HJ Kania as its first CJI.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Indian Constitution mentions that the law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all the courts within the territory of India? [SSC CPO 27/06/2024]
solution:
(C) Article 141
Article 141 - The law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all courts within the territory of India. Article 151 - deals with the Audit Reports of the Union. Article 137 - gives the Supreme Court the power to review its own judgments or orders. Note that the term 'Judicial Review' is not mentioned anywhere in the constitution.

subject: Polity
question_text: Article 126 of the Indian Constitution mentions _______. [SSC GD 27/02/2024]

options.c: Appointment of acting Chief Justice of India
options.d: Supreme Court to be a Court of record

solution:
(C) Appointment of Acting Chief Justice of India
Article 126 - appointment of acting chief justice. Under this, when the CJI is absent or the post is vacant, the President can appoint another Supreme Court judge to act as the CJI. Article 127 - appointment of Ad hoc judges, Article 128 - Retired Supreme Court judges can be asked to serve temporarily in the Supreme Court with the President's consent, Article 129 - SC to be a court of record, Article 130 - seat of the SC.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Supreme Court has special advisory jurisdiction in matters which may specifically be referred to it by the President of India under Article _______ of the Constitution. [SSC GD 07/03/2024]
solution:
(B) 143
Article 143 - Power of the President to consult the Supreme Court (Advisory Jurisdiction). Under this, the President can refer to matters relating to: (i) Pre constitutional treaties - The Supreme Court has to mandatorily give the advice but it is not binding on the President. (ii) General Importance - The Supreme Court may or may not give the advice. The provisions of Advisory Jurisdiction are adopted from the Canadian Constitution.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following are the requisite qualifications of Supreme Court judges? [SSC JE 07/06/2024]
1. He should be a citizen of India.
2. He should have been a High Court judge for five years or should have been a High Court advocate for ten years.
3. He should be a distinguished jurist in the opinion of the President.
4. He should be a minimum of 35 years old.
solution:
(B) Only 1, 2 and 3
The qualification to become a SC Judge is - A person must be a citizen of India or have been a judge of a High Court for at least 5 years or have been an advocate of a High Court for at least 10 years or be a distinguished jurist in the opinion of the President. There is no minimum age to be eligible to become a judge. However, a judge holds office until the age of 65 years.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following organisations said that India CANNOT have a parallel judicial system - one for the rich and the other for the poor? [SSC CHSL 09/07/2024]
solution:
(C) Supreme Court
In the Satender Kumar Antil vs. CBI (2022) case, the Supreme Court observed that India cannot have a parallel judicial system-one for the rich and another for the poor. The Court criticized the disparity in bail practices and emphasized the need for bail reforms and equal access to justice, urging the implementation of guidelines to prevent unnecessary arrests.

subject: Polity
question_text: Under the discretionary power of which Article of the Indian Constitution did the SC of India recognise sex work as a profession? [SSC CHSL 04/07/2024]
solution:
(A) Article 142
Article 142 empowers the Supreme Court to pass any order necessary to do complete justice in any matter pending before it. In Union Carbide Corporation vs. Union of India (1989), SC invoked Article 142 to approve a $470 million settlement between Union Carbide and the Government to provide immediate relief to victims.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements are correct regarding the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India? [SSC CPO 27/06/2024]
I. Original jurisdiction extends to any dispute between the Government of India and one or more States or between the Government of India and any State or States on one side and one or more States on the other or between two or more States.
II. Appellate jurisdiction extends when the Union plus some States are one side and some other States on the other side of the dispute.
III. Under Articles 129 and 142 of the Constitution, the Supreme Court has been vested with the power to punish for contempt of court.
IV. As of now, the International Commercial Arbitration cannot be initiated in the Supreme Court.
solution:
(C) I and III only
The original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court (Article 131) covers disputes: between the Government of India and one or more States, or between the Government of India and any State(s) on one side and one or more other States on the other side, or between two or more States. Statement II is wrong - this falls under original jurisdiction. Articles 129 and 142 grant the Supreme Court the power to punish for contempt of court. Statement IV is wrong - International Commercial Arbitration can be initiated in the Supreme Court under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Supreme Court of India consists of _______ judges and Chief Judges of India. [SSC CPO 28/06/2024]
solution:
(D) 33
Originally, there were 7 judges and CJI. The Parliament of India decides the strength of judges in the Supreme Court through legislation. Now, the strength is of 33 judges and the Chief Justice of India.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which writ is issued by a high court or supreme court when a lower court has considered a case going beyond its jurisdiction? [SSC CGL 26/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Prohibition
Prohibition is issued by a higher court to a lower court to stop it from continuing a case that it has no jurisdiction over. It is preventive in nature. Under Article 32 (Right to Constitutional remedies), the Supreme Court can issue five types of writs: Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari and Quo-Warranto. High Courts can also issue these writs under Article 226 (broader scope, not limited to Fundamental Rights).

subject: Polity
question_text: Who among the following served as the first Chief Justice of India? [SSC CHSL TIER-2 26/06/2023]
solution:
(A) Hiralal Jekisundas Kania
1st CJI - Hiralal Jekisundas Kania, 2nd CJI - M. Patanjali Sastri, 50th CJI - DY Chandrachud, 51st CJI - Sanjiv Khanna

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Indian Constitution mentions that all the authorities _______ civil and judicial- in the territory of India shall act in aid of the Supreme Court? [SSC CPO 05/10/2023]
solution:
(C) Article 144
Article 144 - All authorities civil and judicial, shall act in aid of the Supreme Court. Article 143 : Power of President to Consult Supreme Court. The Court's opinion in this article is advisory and not binding. Article 141 : Law Declared by Supreme Court to Be Binding on All Courts. Article 142 : Enforcement of Decrees and Orders of Supreme Court.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following statements is correct about Article 129 of the Constitution of India? [SSC MTS 01/09/2023]

options.a: It provides for the Supreme Court to give advisory opinions to the President.
options.b: It provides for the Supreme Court to be a court of original jurisdiction.
options.c: It provides for the Supreme Court to be a court of record.
options.d: It provides for the Supreme Court to be the highest court of appeal.

solution:
(C) It provides for the Supreme Court to be a court of record.
For explanation, refer to question 2.

subject: Polity
question_text: Who has the power to interpret the constitution of India? [SSC MTS 08/05/2023]

options.c: Both Supreme court and High courts

solution:
(C) Both Supreme court and High courts
Both the Supreme Court and the High Courts have the power to interpret the Constitution. They can examine the constitutionality of laws and ensure they comply with constitutional provisions.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following provisions of the Indian constitution is borrowed from the Constitution of Canada? [SSC CHSL 09/03/2023]

options.c: Advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme court

solution:
(C) Advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme court
Advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court (Article 143) is borrowed from the Canadian Constitution. Concurrent List - From Australia, Independence of Judiciary - From USA, Concept of Republic - From France.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Constitution of India grants power to the President to appoint Judges of the Supreme Court? [SSC CGL 10/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Article 124(2)
Article 124(2) states that the President shall appoint every Judge of the Supreme Court, including the Chief Justice of India. The appointment is made after consultation with such judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts as the President may deem necessary (collegium system). The Collegium System is a method by which judges appoint judges in India. It includes the Chief Justice of India (CJI) + 4 senior-most Supreme Court judges. They recommend appointments and transfers of judges to the President.

subject: Polity
question_text: In May 2023, the Supreme Court of India granted couples the right to get divorce on the grounds of 'irretrievable breakdown of marriage' under _______ of the Indian Constitution. [SSC CHSL TIER-2 26/06/2023]
solution:
(A) Article 142(1)
Article 142(1) allows the Supreme Court to pass any order necessary to do complete justice in any case before it. This power is extraordinary and allows the Court to override usual legal procedures to ensure justice is served.

subject: Polity
question_text: Provisions related to the seat of the "Supreme Court of India" is mentioned in which Article? [SSC MTS 12/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Article 130
Article 130 of the Indian Constitution states that the seat of the Supreme Court shall be in Delhi, but the Chief Justice of India can allow it to sit at any other place with the approval of the President.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following Articles is related to the Ancillary powers of the Supreme Court? [SSC CGL 06/03/2020]
solution:
(D) Article 140
Article 140 allows the Parliament to confer additional (ancillary) powers on the Supreme Court as may be necessary to enable it to perform its functions effectively. Article 138 - Expansion of jurisdiction of the Supreme Court, Article 143 - Advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court, Article 150 - Accounts of the Union and States.

subject: Polity
question_text: Who appoints the Chief Justice of India? [SSC MTS 12/05/2023]
solution:
(B) President
The CJI is appointed by the President. For further explanation, refer to question 22.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Indian Constitution empowers the Supreme Court to review its own judgements or orders? [SSC CGL 25/07/2023]
solution:
(D) Article 137
Article 137 gives the Supreme Court the power to review its own judgments or orders. Article 135 - Jurisdiction and powers of the Federal Court under the Government of India Act, 1935. Article 136 - Special leave to appeal by the Supreme Court. Article 138 - Enlargement of the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court.

subject: Polity
question_text: Disputes between two or more states come under the _______ of the Supreme Court. [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(A) Original jurisdiction
Disputes between two or more states fall under the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court, as per Article 131 of the Indian Constitution. Appellate jurisdiction (Articles 132, 133, 134) - Hearing appeals from lower courts. Water dispute cases between two or more states in India are handled by the Inter-State Water Disputes Tribunal which is set up under the Inter-State River Water Disputes Act, 1956. Only Parliament can set up such a tribunal (not the Supreme Court) when a dispute arises.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following jurisdictions of the Supreme Court allows it to settle disputes between the centre and state and amongst states? [SSC CGL 13/12/2022]
solution:
(D) Original
The Original Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court, under Article 131, allows it to settle disputes: Between the Centre and one or more States or Between two or more States.

subject: Polity
question_text: A judge of the Supreme Court can be removed only on the grounds of _______. [SSC CGL 09/12/2022]
solution:
(B) Proven misbehaviour or incapacity
Grounds for Removal: Proven misbehavior or incapacity. A removal motion can be initiated in either House of Parliament which needs to be signed by 100 MPs in Lok Sabha or 50 MPs in Rajya Sabha. The Speaker (Lok Sabha) or Chairman (Rajya Sabha) decides whether to admit the motion. If admitted, a 3-member committee of a SC Judge, Chief Justice of HC, a distinguished jurist is formed to investigate. If the judge is found guilty, the motion moves to Parliament. Both Houses must pass the motion with a special majority. If passed, the President signs the order of removal.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which is NOT correct about the independence of the Judiciary in our country? [SSC CPO 11/11/2022]

options.a: The Judiciary has the power to penalise those who are found guilty of contempt of court.
options.b: The Judges are financially dependent on both the executive and legislature for their salaries and allowances.
options.c: The Constitution prescribes a very difficult procedure for removal of judges.
options.d: The legislature is not involved in the process of appointment of judges.

solution:
(B) The judges are financially dependent on both the executive and legislature for their salaries and allowances.
Judges are financially independent. Their salaries and allowances are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India (Article 266) which is not subject to a vote in Parliament ensuring financial security, which is a key part of judicial independence.

subject: Polity
question_text: In which of the following cases the Supreme Court, while laying out the 'basic structure' of the Constitution held that the democratic character of the polity was an essential component of the 'basic structure'? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]

options.a: Romesh Thappar vs. State of Madras
options.b: AK Gopalan vs. State of Madras
options.c: Kesavananda Bharati vs. State of Kerala
options.d: Sajjan Singh vs. State of Rajasthan

solution:
(C) Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala
In the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), the Supreme Court laid down the 'Basic Structure Doctrine', holding that Parliament can amend the Constitution, but cannot alter or destroy its "basic structure". The Court ruled that features like: Democracy, Rule of law, Separation of powers, Judicial review, Fundamental Rights, etc., are part of the basic structure.

subject: Polity
question_text: Certain provisions of the Benami Transactions (Prohibition) Act were struck off by the Supreme Court. In which year was the law originally enacted? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(D) 1988
A benami transaction refers to a situation where a property is purchased in the name of one person, but the consideration (money) is paid by another person. The purpose of the Act was to: Prohibit benami transactions, prevent people from hiding unaccounted wealth in the name of others to evade taxes or conceal black money.

subject: Polity
question_text: Till which year did the High Court of Delhi continue to exercise jurisdiction over Himachal Pradesh? [SSC CGL 27/07/2023]
solution:
(D) 1968
The High Court of Delhi exercised jurisdiction over Himachal Pradesh until 1968, after which the Himachal Pradesh High Court was established on 25th January, 1971.

subject: Polity
question_text: Who holds the authority of transferring judges from one High court to another High Court? [SSC CGL 14/07/2023]

options.a: The senior most judge of Supreme Court

solution:
(C) The President of india
His power is exercised in consultation with the Chief Justice of India. According to Article 222 of the Indian Constitution, the President can transfer a judge from one High Court to another after consulting the Chief Justice of India.

subject: Polity
question_text: In which of the following years was the Bombay High Court of India established? [SSC GD 05/03/2024]
solution:
(C) 1862
The first High Court in India was the Calcutta High Court established on 1st July 1862 under The Indian High Courts Act of 1861. It was followed by the Bombay and Madras High Courts. Bombay High Court - 14th August 1862, Madras High Court - 15th August 1862. As of now, there are 25 High Courts in India.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following was the first High Court established in India? [SSC CHSL 06/08/2021]
solution:
(B) Calcutta
For explanation, refer to question 36. As of now, some High Courts have jurisdiction over multiple regions - (1) Bombay HC - Maharashtra, Goa, Dadra and Nagar Haveli, Daman and Diu, (2) Calcutta HC - West Bengal, Andaman and Nicobar Islands, (3) Kerala HC - Kerala, Lakshadweep, (4) Guwahati HC - Assam, Nagaland, Mizoram, Arunachal Pradesh.

subject: Polity
question_text: Articles 214 to 231 in Part VI of the Indian Constitution deal with the _______. [SSC CGL 23/09/2024]
solution:
(B) High Courts
Articles 214 to 231 in Part VI of the Indian Constitution deal with the High Courts in the states. These articles cover the establishment, jurisdiction, powers, and appointment of judges of High Courts. Article 214 : Establishment of High Courts for states, Article 231 : Establishment of a common High Court for two or more states.

subject: Polity
question_text: The detention of a person cannot exceed _______ unless an advisory board consisting of judges of a High Court reports sufficient cause for extended detention. [SSC CPO 11/11/2022]
solution:
(B) three months
The detention of a person cannot exceed three months unless an advisory board consisting of judges of a High Court reports sufficient cause for extended detention. This is provided in Article 22(4).

subject: Polity
question_text: British Parliament introduced the Indian High Courts Act in which year? [SSC CGL 17/09/2024]
solution:
(C) 1861
The Indian High Courts Act was introduced by the British Parliament in the year 1861. This Act led to the establishment of High Courts in Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras. Also, refer to question 36.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Indian Constitution states that "There shall be a High Court for each state"? [SSC CPO 29/06/2024]
solution:
(C) Article 214
Article 214 - There shall be a High Court, Article 215 - HC to be a court of record, Article 216 - Every High Court shall consist of a Chief Justice and such other judges as the President may from time to time deem it necessary to appoint.

subject: Polity
question_text: As of March 2023, how many High Courts are there in India? [SSC CGL 10/09/2024]
solution:
(D) 25
For explanation, refer to question 36.

subject: Polity
question_text: When was the High Court and Supreme Court Judges (Salaries and Conditions of Service) Amendment Bill, 2021 introduced in Lok Sabha? [SSC CGL 09/09/2024]
solution:
(B) 30 November 2021
The High Court and Supreme Court judges (Salaries and Conditions of Service) Amendment Bill, 2021 was introduced in the Lok Sabha on November 30, 2021.

subject: Polity
question_text: Anna Chandy, the first Indian woman to serve as a judge at a high court was appointed in which High Court? [SSC CGL 11/09/2024]
solution:
(A) Kerala
Anna Chandy, the first Indian woman judge of a High Court was appointed to the Kerala HC on February 9, 1959. Justice M. Fathima Beevi was the first woman judge of the Supreme Court appointed on October 6, 1989.

subject: Polity
question_text: A Supreme Court or High Court judge can be removed by the Parliament by: [SSC CPO 09/11/2022]

options.b: both simple and two third majority

solution:
(C) special majority
As per Article 124(4) for Supreme Court judges, and similarly under Article 217 for High Court judges, a judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court can be removed by the Parliament through a special majority which requires a majority of the total membership of each House of Parliament and a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.

subject: Polity
question_text: As per Article _______ of the Constitution of India, English is the official language for all the high courts of the country. [SSC CGL 17/08/2021]
solution:
(D) 348 (1)
Article 348(1) - language to be used in the Supreme Court and in the High Courts shall be English. It also applies to bills, acts, and ordinances. The provisions of Official Language are contained in Part XVII from Article 343 to 351. Article 343 - official language of the Union (Hindi in Devanagari script). Article 345 - official language of a state (one or more languages in use in the state or Hindi). Article 350A - Instruction in mother tongue (primary education in mother tongue of children belonging to linguistic minorities). Article 350B - Special Officer for linguistic minorities.

subject: Polity
question_text: What is the upper age limit for appointment as a judge of the High court as of October 2022? [SSC MTS 19/06/2023]
solution:
(A) 62 years
Article 217 - Appointment and conditions of the office of a HC judge. (1) Judges are appointed by the President after consultation with the CJI, the governor of the state and for appointments other than the chief justice, the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court. (2) Holds office until the age of 62 years. A judge resigns to the President and has the same procedure for removal as that of a SC judge. (3) Qualifications: Held a judicial office in India for at least ten years, or been an advocate of a High Court (or multiple High Courts in succession) for at least ten years.

subject: Polity
who_can_appoint_the_judges_of_the_high_c: Who can appoint the judges of the High Court? [SSC JE 07/06/2024]
solution:
(A) President
As per Article 217, it is the President who appoints the judge of a High Court after consultation with: The Chief Justice of India, the Governor of the respective State, and for appointments other than that of the Chief Justice, the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.

subject: Polity
question_text: Who was the first Indian woman to be appointed Chief Justice of a state High Court? [SSC CHSL 16/03/2023]
solution:
(A) Leila Seth
In 1991, Justice Leila Seth became the first Indian woman to be appointed as a Chief Justice of a state High Court (Himachal Pradesh HC). Prior to this, she was the first woman judge of Delhi High Court. Anna Chandy was the first Indian woman judge of a High Court (Kerala HC). M Fathima Beevi was the first Indian woman judge of the Supreme Court.

subject: Polity
question_text: The High court of Delhi was established in _______. [SSC MTS 08/05/2023]
solution:
(D) 1966
The High Court of Delhi was established on 31 October, 1966 under the Delhi High Courts Act, 1966.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following statements about the Supreme Court is not correct? [CAPF 2021]

options.a: Under Article 129 and Article 144 of the Constitution of India, the Supreme Court has been vested with power to punish for contempt of court.
options.b: The Supreme Court has been vested with advisory jurisdiction.
options.c: The Supreme Court may refuse to provide opinion to the President, if so asked, in certain circumstances.
options.d: Law declared by the Supreme Court is binding on all courts within the territory of India.

solution:
(A) Under Article 129 and Article 144 of the Constitution of India, the Supreme Court has been vested with power to punish for contempt of court.
Article 129 - Supreme Court to be a court of record and can punish for contempt of itself. However, this power is not vested under Article 144 which mandates all civil and judicial authorities to act in aid of the Supreme Court. Hence, statement 1 is incorrect. Article 143 - advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under which the President can take the opinion of SC on pre constitutional matters or matters of general importance. The advice of the Supreme Court is not binding on the President. Article 141- Law declared by SC to be binding on all the courts.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following powers of the Supreme Court is also conferred on a High Court? [CAPF 2021]

options.b: Power of doing complete justice in any cause or matter pending before the court
options.d: Power to allow/refuse Special Leave Petitions

solution:
(C) Power of court of record
Both Supreme Court (Article 129) and High Court (Article 215) are the courts of record. These records can be used for reference or evidentiary purposes in future cases. Power to allow/refuse Special Leave Petitions - Exclusive to the Supreme Court under Article 136.

subject: Polity
question_text: Who can be appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court? [CAPF 2021]

options.a: An advocate who has practised for at least ten years in any court.
options.b: A judge of a High Court for at least three years.
options.c: A distinguished jurist in the opinion of the President of India.
options.d: A person who has held a judicial office for at least fifteen years.

solution:
(C) A distinguished jurist in the opinion of the President of India.
Qualifications to become a Judge of the Supreme Court are given in Article 124(3). As per this, a member must: (i) be a citizen of India, (ii) Has been a Judge of a High Court for at least 5 years, or has been an advocate of a High Court for at least 10 years, or is, in the opinion of the President, a distinguished jurist.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following is correct in respect of the appointment of District Judges? [CAPF 2022]

options.a: They are appointed by the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.
options.b: They are appointed by the President of India on the advice of the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.
options.c: They are appointed by the Governor in consultation with the High Court of the concerned State.
options.d: They are appointed by the Supreme Court on the advice of the concerned High Court.

solution:
(C) They are appointed by the Governor in consultation with the High Court of the concerned State.
According to Article 233, District court judges are appointed by the governor of the state. This is done in consultation with the High Court of the concerned state.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following statements with regard to the impeachment of a Judge of the Supreme Court of India is not correct? [CAPF 2023]

options.a: A motion addressed to the President, signed by at least 100 members of both the Houses of the Parliament is delivered to the Speaker.
options.b: The motion is investigated by a Committee of three (2 Judges of the Supreme Court and a distinguished jurist).
options.c: If the Committee finds the Judge guilty of misbehaviour or that he suffers from incapacity, the motion together with the report of the Committee is taken up for consideration in the House where the motion is pending.
options.d: The judge will be removed after the President gives his order for removal.

solution:
(A) A motion addressed to the President, signed by at least 100 members of both the Houses of the Parliament is delivered to the Speaker.
The motion is not addressed to the President; it is submitted to the Speaker of the Lok Sabha or the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, depending on where it is introduced. For Lok Sabha, the motion must be signed by at least 100 members. For Rajya Sabha, the motion must be signed by at least 50 members. Importantly, the Speaker or Chairman can either admit or reject the motion. If the motion is not admitted, the process stops right there. For further information, refer to question 30. Both the Supreme Court and High court judges have the same process for removal.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which among the following statements regarding the powers of the High Courts under Article 226 of the Constitution of India is not correct? [CAPF 2023]

options.a: It can issue writs of habeas corpus, mandamus, quo warranto, certiorari, and prohibition.
options.b: Writs can be issued to enforce any rights conferred by Part-III and for any other purpose.
options.c: This power can derogate the power conferred on the Supreme Court under Article 32(2).
options.d: Writ can be issued to any authority under its jurisdiction.

solution:
(C) This power can derogate the power conferred on the Supreme Court under Article 32(2).
High Courts under Article 226 can issue writs: habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto, and certiorari not only for enforcement of Fundamental Rights (Part III) but also for the legal rights. Article 226 does not derogate (i.e., reduce or override) the power of the Supreme Court under Article 32(2). In fact, both powers coexist, and Article 32 is itself a Fundamental Right, while Article 226 is a constitutional power of the High Court. Hence, C is incorrect. High Courts can issue writs to any person or authority within its territorial jurisdiction.

subject: Polity
question_text: Article 231 of the Constitution of India grants power to establish a common High Court for two or more states to: [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(A) the Parliament.
Article 231 - empowers Parliament to establish a common High Court for two or more states and a UT. Other articles : (Trick - mHRC AAO ReSt) Article 214 - High Courts for States, Article 215 - High Courts to be courts of record, Article 216 - Constitution of High Courts, Article 217 - Appointment and conditions of the office of a judge of a High Court, Article 218 - Application of certain provisions relating to the Supreme Court to High Courts, Article 219 - Oath or affirmation by judges of High Courts, Article 220 - Restriction on practice after being a permanent Judge, Article 221 - Salaries, etc., Article 222 - Transfer of a Judge from one High Court to another, Article 226 - Power to issue writs.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following statements is not correct? [CDS II 2021]

options.a: Allahabad High Court has a Bench at Lucknow.
options.b: Madhya Pradesh High Court has a Bench at Gwalior.
options.c: Rajasthan High Court is located at Jodhpur.
options.d: Guwahati High Court has no Bench.

solution:
(D) Guwahati High Court has no Bench.
The Guwahati High Court does have benches in other northeastern states like Nagaland (Kohima), Mizoram (Aizawl), and Arunachal Pradesh (Itanagar). Allahabad High Court has a Bench at Lucknow. Madhya Pradesh High Court has benches at Gwalior and Indore. Rajasthan High Court is located at Jodhpur.

subject: Polity
question_text: The landmark Supreme Court judgement in the case Subhash Sharma vs. Union of India (1991) refers to which one of the following basic features of the Constitution of India? [CDS II 2023]
solution:
(A) Judicial Review
The Subhash Sharma vs. Union of India (1991) case emphasized judicial review and the independence of the judiciary as part of the basic structure of the Constitution, especially in the context of judicial appointments. This case laid the groundwork for the later Second Judges Case (1993), which led to the Collegium System.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following does not fall under the jurisdiction of the apex court of India? [NDA I 2022]
solution:
(C) Collegiate Jurisdiction
(1) Original Jurisdiction - Article 131 (disputes between Centre and States), (2) Appellate Jurisdiction - Supreme Court hears appeals in (i) Constitutional Matters under Article 132, (ii) Civil Matters under Article 133, (iii) Criminal matters under Article 134, (3) Advisory Jurisdiction - Under Article 143, the President can seek the Supreme Court's advice. Collegiate Jurisdiction, hence C is incorrect.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Supreme Court of India exercises advisory jurisdiction under which Article of the Constitution of India? [CBI 2023]
solution:
(C) Article 143
Advisory jurisdiction is provided under Article 143. For explanation, refer to question 10 of level I.

subject: Polity
question_text:
With reference to India, consider the following statements:
1. Judicial custody means that an accused is in the custody of the concerned magistrate and such accused is locked up in police station, not in jail.
2. During judicial custody, the police officer in charge of the case is not allowed to interrogate the suspect without the approval of the court.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct? [IAS 2021]
solution:
(B) 2 only
Judicial custody means that the accused is in the custody of the magistrate and is kept in jail but not in police lock-up. During judicial custody, the police cannot interrogate the accused without permission from the court. This protects the rights of the accused and limits police access.

subject: Polity
question_text:
With reference to Indian judiciary, consider the following statements:
1. Any retired judge of the Supreme Court of India can be called back to sit and act as a Supreme Court judge by the Chief Justice of India with prior permission of the President of India.
2. A High Court in India has the power to review its own judgement as the Supreme Court does.
Which of the statements given above are correct? [IAS 2021]
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
Under Article 128, a retired judge of the Supreme Court can be requested by the Chief Justice of India, with the prior consent of the President, to sit and act as a judge of the Supreme Court. A High Court has the power to review its own judgment, similar to the Supreme Court.

subject: Polity
question_text:
With reference to the writs issued by the Courts in India, consider the following statements:
1. Mandamus will not lie against a private organisation unless it is entrusted with a public duty.
2. Mandamus will not lie against a Company even though it may be a Government Company.
3. Any public minded person can be a petitioner to move the Court to obtain the writ of Quo Warranto.
Which of the statements given above are correct? [IAS 2022]
solution:
(C) 1 and 3 only
Mandamus is issued to compel a public authority to perform a duty. It cannot be issued to a private organization unless it is performing a public duty but it can lie against a Government Company if it is performing public functions or duties. Being a government-owned company does not automatically exempt it from writ jurisdiction. In the case of Quo Warranto, any public-spirited person can file a petition to challenge the illegal occupation of a public office, even if they are not personally affected.

subject: Polity
question_text:
With reference to India, consider the following statements:
1. Government law officers and legal firms are recognised as advocates, but corporate lawyers and patent attorneys are excluded from recognition as advocates.
2. Bar Councils have the power to lay down the rules relating to legal education and recognition of law colleges.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct? [IAS 2022]
solution:
(B) 2 only
Corporate Lawyers, as well as patent attorneys, are too recognized as lawyers and there's no prohibition on their recognition as lawyers. Hence, statement 1 is incorrect. Bar Councils in India, established under the Advocates Act, 1961, serve as statutory bodies to regulate the legal profession and legal education in the country. Hence, statement 2 is correct. There is one Bar Council of India (BCI) at the national level and separate State Bar Councils for each state.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Pursuant to the report of H.N. Sanyal Committee, the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971 was passed.
2. The Constitution of India empowers the Supreme Court and the High Courts to punish for contempt of themselves.
3. The Constitution of India defines Civil Contempt and Criminal Contempt.
4. In India, the Parliament is vested with the powers to make laws on Contempt of Court.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct? [IAS 2022]
solution:
(B) 1, 2 and 4
The Contempt of Courts Act, 1971 was enacted based on the H.N. Sanyal Committee report. The Supreme Court (Art. 129) and High Courts (Art. 215) have constitutional powers to punish for contempt. While Parliament can make laws on contempt, the definitions of civil and criminal contempt are given in the 1971 Act, not in the Constitution itself.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Match the following committees with their contributions to local governance and select the correct answer using the codes/options given below the lists:
List-I (Committee)
A. Ashok Mehta Committee
B. G.V.K. Rao
C. L.M. Singhvi Committee
D. Sarkaria Commission

List-II (Contribution)
i. Recommended that Panchayati Raj institutions should be constitutionally recognized, protected and preserved.
ii. Suggested that local government should be given constitutional status.
iii. Proposed a two-tier Panchayati Raj System.
iv. Reviewed the working of the Constitution in relation to the working of the Panchayats and Municipalities
[IB ACIO 18/01/2024]
solution:
(B) A-iii B-iv C-i D-ii
Ashok Mehta Committee, 1977 - iii) Proposed a two-tier Panchayati Raj System; G.V.K. Rao Committee, 1985 - iv) Reviewed the working of the Constitution in relation to the working of the Panchayats and Municipalities; L.M. Singhvi Committee, 1986 - ii) Suggested that local government should be given constitutional status ; Sarkaria Commission, 1983 - i) Recommended that Panchayati Raj institutions should be constitutionally recognized, protected and preserved.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following committees was/were set up by the government for local bodies in India? [IB ACIO 18/01/2024]
1. Balwant Rai Mehta Committee
2. The Ashok Mehta Committee
3. Khusro Committee
solution:
(B) Only 1 and 2
Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957) recommended the three tier Panchayati System at the - 1. Village Level - Gram Panchayat, 2. Block Level - Panchayat Samiti, 3. District Level - Zila Parishad. This system was aimed at decentralized planning and development from the grassroots. On the other hand, the Ashok Mehta Committee (1977), recommended the two tier Panchayati Raj System at the - 1. District Level - Zila Parishad, 2. Mandal Level - Mandal Panchayat. The Ashok Mehta Committee did not recommend an intermediate level. Khusro Committee - This committee, officially known as the National Commission on Urbanisation (1986), focused on urban development and urban local governance, and not on the local bodies.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which part of the Constitution deals with the Panchayats? [SSC CGL 23/09/2024]
solution:
(B) Part IX
The 73rd CA, 1992 gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) in India. It came into effect on 24 April 1993. Key provisions added by it were - (1) Part IX - "The Panchayats" under which Articles 243 to 243O were inserted. (2) 11th Schedule - Lists 29 subjects under Panchayat jurisdiction. Examples: Agriculture, rural housing, drinking water, roads, poverty alleviation, education, etc.

subject: Polity
question_text: How many tiers are there in a Panchayati Raj structure? [SSC CPO 09/12/2019]
solution:
(A) Three
Three-tier structure of Panchayati Raj for states: (1) Gram Panchayat - Village level, (2) Panchayat Samiti - Block/intermediate level, (3) Zila Parishad - District level. However, the three tier system is not mandatory in every state as the states with population less than 20 lakhs have a two-tier Panchayati Raj system (without the intermediate level).

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following states is one of those states that have no Panchayati Raj Institution at all? [SSC CGL 25/09/2024]
solution:
(C) Nagaland
Nagaland does not have a Panchayati Raj System due to its special provisions under Article 371(A). Also, Article 243M states that Part IX does not apply to certain areas and requires modifications to be applied in the scheduled areas. To ensure self governance for tribal communities, the PESA Act, 1996 (Panchayats Extension to Scheduled Areas) was enacted to scheduled areas under the 5th schedule. States without Panchayati Raj Institutions are Nagaland, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Some tribal areas of Assam and Tripura, all UTs except Delhi. These areas are governed by Autonomous District Councils under the Sixth Schedule, not the 73rd Amendment.

subject: Polity
question_text: Provisions related to the Municipalities are mentioned under which part of the Indian constitution? [SSC MTS 16/06/2023]
solution:
(A) Part IXA
Part IXA deals with the Municipalities from Article 243P to 243ZG. Lord Mayo's Resolution - 1870 focused on the decentralization of financial administration. It encouraged local bodies to take up works like roads, education, sanitation, etc. Also, the Lord Ripon's Resolution - 1882 focused on the Democratic local self-government. It is known as the Magna Carta of Local Self-Government in India. (Lord Ripon - Father of local self government). It advocated for elected members in local bodies and promoted district boards and municipal boards.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution mentions about the organisation of "Village Panchayats"? [SSC CGL 10/09/2024]
solution:
(B) Article 40
Article 40 of the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP) in Part IVA states that the State shall take steps to organize village panchayats and endow them with such powers and authority as may be necessary to enable them to function as units of self-government. It is a Gandhian Principle. The Panchayati Raj System was first introduced in Rajasthan, Nagaur on 2nd October, 1959 followed by Andhra Pradesh on 11th October, 1959.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Gram Sabha is a meeting of all adults who live in the area covered by a _______. [SSC MTS 20/06/2023]
solution:
(D) Panchayat
The Gram Sabha is the assembly of all adult members (18 years and above) living in a village that falls under a Gram Panchayat.

subject: Polity
question_text: A Panchayat continues for _______ from the date appointed for its first meeting. [SSC MTS 18/10/2021]
solution:
(C) 5 years
Article 243E - A Panchayat shall continue for a term of five years from the date appointed for its first meeting, unless dissolved earlier.

subject: Polity
question_text: 73rd Amendment Act 1992 makes provisions for a 3-tier system of Panchayati Raj for all the states having population of above: [SSC PHASE XII 26/06/2024]
solution:
(C) 20 lakhs
The three-tier system of Panchayati Raj: (1) Gram Panchayat - Village level, (2) Panchayat Samiti - Intermediate/Block level, (3) Zila Parishad - District level. However, the intermediate (middle) tier is not mandatory for states with a population of less than 20 lakh. The three-tier Panchayati Raj system was first recommended by the Balwant Rai Mehta Committee in 1957.

subject: Polity
question_text: The elections to the Panchayati Raj institutions are conducted by the _______. [SSC CGL 05/12/2022]
solution:
(C) State Election Commission
Elections to the Panchayati Raj Institutions (like Gram Panchayats, Panchayat Samitis, and Zila Parishads) are conducted by the State Election Commission, as per Article 243K. The State Election Commission is an autonomous constitutional authority headed by the state election commissioner appointed by the Governor and consists of other members, depending on the state's legislation. Their conditions of service and tenure are also determined by the Governor, subject to state laws. Also, Article 243E states that the duration of a Panchayat shall be 5 years.

subject: Polity
question_text: The election to constitute a Panchayat should be completed before the expiration of a period of _______ from the date of its dissolution. [SSC CHSL 14/10/2020]
solution:
(C) Six months
Article 243E(3) - An election to constitute a Panchayat shall be completed before the expiration of a period of six months from the date of its dissolution.

subject: Polity
question_text: Article 243K of the Indian constitution is related with which of the following? [SSC CHSL 20/03/2023]

options.b: Grounds of disqualification from membership of Panchayat.

solution:
(D) Elections to the Panchayats
Article 243K provides for the establishment of a state election commission responsible for conducting elections to the Panchayats. The conditions of service and tenure are also determined by the Governor. The Commissioner cannot be removed except in the manner and on the grounds prescribed for a High Court judge (to ensure independence). Article 243 -definitions related to panchayat, 243A - Gram Sabha Assembly, Article 243B - Constitution of Panchayats (3 tier system), 243C - Composition of Panchayat, 243D - Reservation of seats (SCs, STs, women), 243E -Duration, 243F - Disqualifications for membership, 243G - Powers, authorities and responsibilities of Panchayats, 243H - Power to impose taxes and receive funds, 243I - Constitution of state finance commission, 243J - Audit of accounts, 243K - Elections.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which among the following is INCORRECT about the salient features of the 73rd Amendment Act? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]

options.a: The Act corresponds to Part III of the Constitution of India.
options.b: The Act gives a Constitutional status to the Panchayati Raj Institutions.
options.c: The Act has added the Eleventh Schedule to the Constitution of India.
options.d: It contains 29 functional items of the Panchayats and deals with Article 243 to 243(O).

solution:
(A) The Act corresponds to Part III of the Constitution of India.
The 73rd CA, 1992 deals with the addition of Part IX and schedule 11th. Part III deals with the Fundamental Rights which were originally present in the constitution. For further explanation, refer to question 3 and 4.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Constitution Amendment Act added provisions related to Municipalities in the Indian Constitution? [SSC CHSL 16/03/2023]
solution:
(C) 74th Amendment Act 1992
The 74th CA, 1992 added provisions related to Municipalities (Urban Local Bodies). It came into effect on 1st June 1993. It added Part IXA (Articles 243P to 243ZG) and the 12th schedule listing 18 functional subjects to the Constitution. 3 types of Municipalities: Nagar Panchayat (for transitional areas); Municipal Council (for smaller urban areas) ; Municipal Corporation (for larger urban areas). First municipal corporation - Madras, 1688. Second - Bombay and Calcutta, 1726.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following articles is related to the Provision of Cooperative Societies in the Indian Constitution? [SSC CHSL 10/08/2023]
solution:
(A) 243 ZH to 243 ZT
Part IXB deals with Co-operative Societies. It was added by the 97th CA Act, 2011. It also added Article 19(c) and Article 43B. Part IXB includes Articles 243ZH to 243ZT, which provide the legal framework for: Incorporation, Regulation, Governance, Elections, Audit, Supersession, Rights of members. Elections to be conducted by State Cooperative Election Authorities and the Board tenure is limited to 5 years.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Constitution (74th Amendment) Act, 1992 provides for the constitution of Wards Committees.
2. The Twelfth Schedule of the Constitution lists the powers, authority, and responsibilities of Municipalities.
3. Urban Local Governments in India include Municipal Corporations, Municipal Councils, and Nagar Panchayats depending on the size of the population.
Which of the above statements are correct? [IB ACIO 17/01/2024]
solution:
(A) 1, 2 and 3
The 74th Amendment Act, 1992 provides for Wards Committees in cities with over 3 lakh population (Art. 243S) and added the 12th Schedule, listing 18 functions of Municipalities. Urban local bodies in India-Municipal Corporations (large cities), Municipal Councils (smaller urban areas), and Nagar Panchayats (transitional areas from rural to urban)-are classified based on population size.

subject: Polity
question_text: If the State government dissolves the Panchayati Raj Institutions before the end of their five-year term, fresh elections should ordinarily be held within _______. [SSC CGL 12/12/2022]
solution:
(B) six months
As per Article 243E(3), the State Election Commission must conduct elections within 6 months of dissolution. Exception - If the remaining period of the term is less than 6 months, then elections are not mandatory. If elections are held (and time remaining > 6 months), the new Panchayat will serve only the remaining term of the dissolved Panchayat - not a full 5 years.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Constitution of India contains no provision for the constitution of municipalities in every State for: [CAPF 2021]
solution:
(D) an Urban Panchayat
The Constitution (74th Amendment Act, 1992) provides for three types of Municipalities under Article 243Q: (1) Nagar Panchayat - for areas in transition from rural to urban, (2) Municipal Council - for smaller urban areas, (3) Municipal Corporation - for larger urban areas. There is no such term as "Urban Panchayat" in the Constitution. The are other statutory or administrative bodies of the urban local government created under respective state or central laws (8 in total). (4)Town Area Committee, (5) Notified Area Committee, (6) Cantonment Board (under Ministry of Defence), (7) Township (set up by public sector undertakings), (8) Port Trust (for port towns).

subject: Polity
question_text: Under which one among the following Articles of the Constitution of India, the seats are reserved for the Scheduled Caste and the Scheduled Tribe in every Municipality? [CAPF 2024]
solution:
(C) 243-T
Article 243T deals with reservation of seats proportional to their population in the municipal area in every Municipality for: Scheduled Castes (SCs), Scheduled Tribes (STs), Women (including SC/ST and women). It also mandates that not less than one-third of the total number of seats be reserved for women. 243R - Composition of Municipalities, 243S - Constitution and composition of Wards Committees, 243ZA - Conduct of elections to the Municipalities.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements about the Panchayati Raj System are correct?
1. All seats in a Panchayat are filled by persons chosen by direct election.
2. The 'Gram Sabha' consists of persons registered in the village electoral rolls.
3. The Chairperson of a Panchayat is elected in accordance with a law passed by each state.
4. All states in India have a three-tier system of Panchayats.
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(B) 1, 2 and 3 only
The members to all the levels of the Panchayati Raj are elected directly and the chairpersons to the intermediate and the district level are elected indirectly from the elected members and at the village level, the Chairperson is elected as determined by the state government as per Article 243 C (Composition of panchayats).Also, states with a population of fewer than 20 lakhs may not constitute the intermediate level. Hence, 4 is incorrect.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. The elections to the Panchayat are conducted by the State Election Commission.
2. The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Chief Election Commissioner of India.
3. The State Legislatures have the power to make laws on all matters relating to Panchayat elections.
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(D) 1 and 3 only
Elections to the Panchayat are conducted by the State Election Commission (as per Article 243K). The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Governor of the state and the State Legislatures have the power to make laws on matters relating to Panchayat elections (under Article 243K).

subject: Polity
question_text: Under which Article of the Constitution of India has provision been made for reservation of seats for women in Panchayats? [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(C) Article 243D
Article 243D deals with the reservation of seats in Panchayats for - (1) Scheduled Castes (SCs), Scheduled Tribes (STs) - in proportion to their population in the Panchayat area. (2) Women - Not less than one-third of the total seats (including the office of the Chairperson) are reserved for women in all levels of Panchayats. 106th CA provides for reservation to women for 1/3rd of the seats.

subject: Polity
question_text: Panchayati Raj Institutions are primarily the institutions of _______. [NDA I 2022]
solution:
(B) Self-Government
Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) are a system of local self-government in rural India, enabling people to participate directly in decision-making and governance. As per Article 243F - A person must be at least 21 years old to contest Panchayat elections and at least 18 years old to be able to vote. The members are directly elected by the people through elections. The State Legislature decides the method of election for Chairpersons under Article 243C(5).

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following provisions of the Constitution of India deals with District Planning Committee? [NDA II 2023]
solution:
(A) 243ZD
Article 243ZD of the Indian Constitution provides for the District Planning Committee (DPC). It ensures that plans prepared by Panchayats and Municipalities are consolidated and integrated into a district-level development plan. The majority of members in the DPC are elected from among the elected members of the Panchayats and Municipalities in the district. Other options : 243ZB - Inapplicability to Certain Areas like: scheduled areas under the Fifth Schedule, hill areas in Manipur; 243ZC - Committee for Small Urban Areas; 243ZE - Metropolitan Planning Committee which deals with the formation of Metropolitan Planning Committees (MPCs) in areas (population >10 lakh) to prepare development plans.

subject: Polity
question_text: The provision for one-third representation for women in the local self-government has been introduced in the Constitution of India through: [CBI 2023]
solution:
(C) 73rd Amendment Act
73rd Amendment Act (1992) - Introduced Part IX (Panchayats) and the Eleventh Schedule. Inserted Article 243D(3) mandating one-third reservation for women (including chairpersons) in all three tiers of Panchayati Raj. Also, 106th CA extended the one-third women's reservation to Municipalities (Part IXA) by amending Article 243T. It also provided for the reservation in Parliament and Legislative Assemblies.

subject: Polity
question_text: Gram Sabha means a body consisting of persons registered: [CISF AC 2021]

options.a: in the electoral rolls relating to a village comprised within the area of Panchayat at the village level.
options.b: in the electoral rolls of wards within the area of Panchayat.
options.c: in the electoral rolls of civic bodies within the area of Panchayat.
options.d: in the electoral rolls of several villages within a local body.

solution:
(A) In the electoral rolls relating to a village comprised within the area of Panchayat at the village level.
Under Article 243A of the Constitution, the Gram Sabha is defined as the body consisting of all persons registered in the electoral rolls for the village (or group of villages) within the area of a Gram Panchayat.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following statements about Panchayati Raj Institutions in India is not correct? [CISF AC 2023]

options.a: Not less than one-third of the total number of the offices of Chairpersons in the Panchayats at each level are reserved for women.
options.b: Reservation for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes are provided for at all the three levels of local bodies.
options.c: States can provide for reservations for the Other Backward Classes (OBCs), if needed.
options.d: State Election Commissioner is appointed by the President of India on the advice of the Governor to conduct Panchayat elections.

solution:
(D) State Election Commissioner is appointed by the President of India on the advice of the Governor to conduct Panchayat elections.
The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Governor of the state (Article 243K), not by the President. Under Article 243D, seats for SCs and STs are reserved at all three tiers, at least one-third of Panchayat posts at each level are reserved for women, and empowers the Legislature of a State to make provisions for reservation of seats in favor of backward classes of citizens in the Panchayats.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements regarding the Panchayat system is/are correct? [GEOSCIENTIST 2023]
1. The Constitution of India envisages a three-tier system of Panchayat.
2. Intermediate Panchayat stands between the village and district Panchayats in the State where the population is above 20 lakhs.
3. All the seats in a Panchayat shall be filled by direct election from territorial constituencies in the Panchayat area.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
solution:
(D) 1, 2 and 3
Article 243B - Constitution of Panchayats (three-tier)-Gram Panchayat, Panchayat Samiti, and Zila Parishad-in every State with a population over two million. In the case of a state with a population less than 2 million, the intermediate level is not there. Article 243C - Composition of Panchayats in which all seats are to be filled by direct election from territorial constituencies within the Panchayat's area. The "all seats" refers to the ward-level seats filled by direct election. The Chairperson posts are not "seats", they're offices. The Chairpersons in many States are elected indirectly by the elected members [as permitted by Article 243C(5)].

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following statements relating to the duration of Panchayat is not correct? [GEOSCIENTIST 2023]

options.a: Every Panchayat shall continue for six years from the date of its first meeting.
options.b: A Panchayat can be dissolved before the expiry of its term in accordance with the procedure prescribed by State Law.
options.c: Election to Panchayat must take place within six months of its dissolution.
options.d: A Panchayat reconstituted after premature dissolution shall continue only for a remainder of the period.

solution:
(A) Every Panchayat shall continue for six years from the date of its first meeting.
As per Article 243E, the term of Panchayat is for five years. For further explanation, refer to question 18.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Constitution (Seventy-third Amendment) Act provides for [NDA I 2025]

options.a: an elaborate system of establishing municipal self-government
options.b: an elaborate system of establishing panchayats as units of self-government
options.c: establishing a Commission for Linguistic Minorities
options.d: the creation of the Jharkhand State

solution:
(B) an elaborate system of establishing panchayats as units of self-government
The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992 is related to Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs). Amendment added Part IX to the Constitution, titled "The Panchayats," and introduced the Eleventh Schedule, which lists the powers, authority, and responsibilities of Panchayats. Similarly, 74th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992, which added Part IX-A to the Constitution, titled "The Municipalities," and the Twelfth Schedule. The state of Jharkhand was created by the Bihar Reorganisation Act, 2000.

subject: Polity
question_text: What is the tenure of the Election Commissioner of India? [SSC CHSL 05/08/2022]
solution:
(C) Six Years
The tenure of an election commissioner is six years or until the age of 65 years, whichever comes earlier. This term applies to both the chief election commissioner and the election commissioners. The number of other election commissioners is decided by the President and they enjoy equal powers and duties as the Chief Election Commissioner as per Article 324. The CEC can only be removed in the same manner and on the same grounds of proven misbehavior or incapacity as a judge of the Supreme Court. However, the President can remove an Election Commissioner, only if the CEC recommends it. First CEC - Sukumar Sen.

subject: Polity
question_text: In which year did the Election Commission of India for the first time in its history become a multi member body? [SSC MTS 28/06/2023]
solution:
(D) 1989
The Election Commission of India became a multi-member body for the first time in the year 1989. Originally, it had only one Chief Election Commissioner. But after the 61st CA, 1988 reduced the minimum age for voting in elections to the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies from 21 years to 18 years, two additional Election Commissioners were appointed, making it a three-member body.

subject: Polity
question_text: Election Commission of India is a _______. [SSC GD 05/03/2024]
solution:
(A) Constitutional body
The Election Commission of India is a constitutional body under Part XV from Article 324 to 329. Related Articles are: Article 324 - Superintendence, direction and control of elections to be vested in an Election Commission, Article 325 - no person can be excluded from the electoral roll or claim to be included in a special roll based on religion, race, caste, or sex; Article 326 - Elections to the Lok Sabha and to the Legislative Assemblies of States to be based on adult suffrage.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements about the Chief Election Commission are correct? [IB ACIO 17/01/2024]
1. The Election Commission celebrated its Golden Jubilee in 2001.
2. It currently consists of Chief Election Commissioner and two Election Commissioners.
3. For the first time two additional Commissioners were appointed in 1993.
4. The President appoints Chief Election Commissioner and Election Commissioners and have tenure of six years or up to the age of 65 years, whichever is earlier.
solution:
(B) Only 1, 2 and 4
The Election Commission celebrated its Golden Jubilee in 2001. It was established on 25th January, 1950 (celebrated as National Voters Day). It currently consists of the Chief Election Commissioner and two Election Commissioners. It became a multi-member body for the first time in 1989. The President appoints CEC and the other two ECs and they have tenure of six years or up to the age of 65 years, whichever is earlier.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article mentions the Comptroller and Auditor General of India? [SSC CGL 24/09/2024]
solution:
(B) Article 148
The CAG is a single member body heading the Indian Audit and Accounts Department. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called the CAG the "Guardian of the Public Purse". It is a constitutional body contained in Part V, Chapter V from Article 148 to 151. He is appointed by the President and serves a tenure of 6 years or until the age of 65 years, whichever is earlier and his salary is decided by the Parliament. The process and the grounds (proven misbehavior or incapacity) for removal of a CAG are the same as a Judge of the Supreme Court. The CAG is not eligible for reappointment. First CAG - V Narhari Rao. Article 148 - Appointment, oath, and conditions of service of the CAG, Article 149 - Duties and powers of the CAG, Article 150 - Form of accounts of the Union and of the States, Article 151 - Audit reports of the CAG.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Finance Commission of India consists of a chairman and [SSC GD 05/03/2024]
solution:
(A) 4 members
The Finance Commission is a constitutional body under Article 280 of Part XII (Finance, Property, Contract and Suits). The President constitutes the Finance Commission every five years. It consists of: 1 Chairman and 4 other members. The Parliament determines the qualifications and eligibility of the members. Its key functions are - recommendation to the President (not binding), distribution of net proceeds of taxes between the Centre and the State. First FC Chairman - KC Neogy, 16th FC Chairman - Aravind Pangaria.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Indian Constitution mentions that 'in the performance of his/her duties, the Attorney-General for India shall have the right of audience in all the courts in the territory of India'? [SSC PHASE XII 02/06/2024]
solution:
(A) Article 76(3)
Article 76 - Attorney-General for India. (1) The President appoints a person who is qualified to be appointed a Judge of the Supreme Court to be Attorney-General for India. (2) The Attorney-General has to give advice to the Government of India upon legal matters, and perform other duties from time to time assigned to him by the President. (3) In the performance of his duties, the Attorney-General shall have the right of audience in all courts in the territory of India. (4) holds office during the pleasure of the President, and receives such remuneration as the President may determine.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following articles of Indian Constitution is related to the Election Commission of India? [SSC GD 30/03/2024]
solution:
(D) Article 324
Article 324 - Superintendence, direction and control of elections to the Parliament, State Legislatures and offices of the President and the Vice-President to be vested in an Election Commission. It consists of a CEC as the Chairman and two ECs appointed by the President. The CEC can be removed only on the grounds of proven misbehavior or incapacity and has the same process of removal as a Supreme Court judge. Provided further that any other EC can be removed by the President only after the recommendation of the CEC.

subject: Polity
question_text: In which Article does the Finance Commission of India find a mention? [SSC CPO 27/06/2024]
solution:
(B) Article 280
Article 280 provides for the constitution, composition, and functions of the Finance Commission. It is contained in Part XII constituted by the President every 5 years. The composition is decided by the Parliament and has a Chairman along with 4 members. The qualifications (decided by the Parliament) are - (i) The Chairman shall have experience in Public Affairs, (ii) The other four members should be qualified in fields such as: Finance and accounts of the government, Administration and governance, Economics, Law or related subjects. He shall be qualified to be a judge of a HC. The members are eligible for re-appointment. Article 281- Recommendations of the Finance Commission.

subject: Polity
question_text: Who was the first chairman of the Finance Commission of India? [SSC CPO 03/10/2023]
solution:
(B) KC Neogy
The first finance commission was constituted on 22 November, 1951 under the Chairmanship of KC Neogy. 16th Finance Commission (2026 - 2031) - Arvind Pangariya. For further information, refer to question 9.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the Finance Commission of India? [SSC CGL 20/07/2023]
A. The Finance Commission consists of a Chairman and four other members.
B. The recommendations made by the Finance Commission are binding on the government and government needs to grant funds according to the advice of the Commission.
C. Article 280 of the Indian Constitution talks about the recommendations of the Finance Commission.
solution:
(C) A only
The recommendations of the Finance Commission are not binding in nature. The Government may accept or reject them. It's Article 281 which deals with the recommendations of the Finance Commission. Under this, after the Finance Commission submits its report: The President must present: (1) The recommendations, and (2) An explanatory memorandum (explaining what action has been taken) to both Houses of Parliament. This ensures transparency and accountability in financial matters between the Centre and the States.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Article of the Indian Constitution mentions about the recommendations of the Finance Commission? [SSC PHASE XII 24/06/2024]
solution:
(A) Article 281
For explanation, refer to question 11.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. The National Commission for Women is a constitutional body.
2. The National Commission for Backward Classes is not a constitutional body.
