Select the correct answer using the code given below: [CBI 2023]
solution:
(D) Neither 1 nor 2
The National Commission for Women is a statutory body established in 1992 under the National Commission for Women Act, 1990. Hence, statement 1 is incorrect. The 102nd CA, 2018 gave constitutional status to the National Commission for Backward Classes under Article 338B of Part XVI. Previously, Article 338 had a Special Officer for SCs and STs, which was replaced by the 65th CA, 1990 by a multi-member National Commission for SCs and STs. Later, the 89th Amendment (2003) split it into two separate commissions: NC for SCs (Article 338), NC for STs (Article 338A).

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following statements about the Finance Commission is not correct? [CISF AC 2021]

options.a: It is a Constitutionally mandated body, set up under Article 280 of the Constitution of India.
options.b: The 14th Finance Commission was set up in 2017 against the backdrop of the introduction of GST.
options.c: Its core responsibility is to evaluate the state of finances of the Union and the State Governments.
options.d: The first Finance Commission was set up in 1951.

solution:
(B) The 14th Finance Commission was set up in 2017 against the backdrop of the introduction of GST.
The Finance Commission is established by the President every five years under Article 280 of the Constitution. The 14th Finance Commission was constituted in 2013 and submitted its report in 2015. GST was introduced in 2017, but the 15th Finance Commission (constituted in 2017) dealt with post-GST finance dynamics. The first Finance Commission was set up in 1951 under the chairmanship of K.C. Neogy. For further explanation, refer to question 9 and 11.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which among the following is not the power of the National Commission for the Scheduled Castes? [CISF AC 2021]

options.a: To investigate all matters to safeguard the Scheduled Castes
options.b: To inquire into specific complaints with respect to deprivation of rights of the Scheduled Castes
options.c: To prepare reports pertaining to the welfare of the Scheduled Castes
options.d: To initiate a judicial enquiry related to the matters of the Scheduled Castes

solution:
(D) To initiate a judicial enquiry related to the matters of the Scheduled Castes
The Commission does not have judicial powers. It can recommend inquiries and seek reports but cannot initiate a judicial proceeding like a court. Originally, a single body under Article 338 was created by the 65th CA, 1990 to look after both Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs). Later, the 89th CA, 2003 bifurcated this into National Commission for SCs (NCSC) - Article 338 and National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (NCST) - Article 338A. The separate NCSC came into effect from February 2004.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following statements is not true in regard to the National Human Rights Commission? [CISF AC 2022]

options.a: It can inquire suo motu on a petition.
options.b: It is a body of the Government of India.
options.c: It can review any matter including acts of terrorism that inhibit the enjoyment of Human Rights.

solution:
(B) It is a body of the Government of India.
The NHRC is a statutory body, created by an Act of Parliament (Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993). It is autonomous, not a government department. The NHRC can take up cases on its own (suo motu), besides receiving complaints and review any matter including acts of terrorism that inhibit human rights. It spreads Human Rights literacy and review constitutional and legal safeguards for human rights.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following is not among the functions of the Election Commission of India? [CISF AC 2022]

options.a: To conduct elections to the Parliament and State Legislatures.
options.b: To supervise, direct and control the preparations of the electoral rolls in a State.
options.c: To conduct elections to the offices of the President and the Vice-President.
options.d: To conduct elections to local bodies like municipalities etc.

solution:
(D) To conduct elections to local bodies like municipalities etc.
The Election Commission of India does not conduct elections to local bodies - this is handled by State Election Commissions. Local body elections (such as to municipalities and panchayats) are conducted by the State Election Commissions, as per Article 243K (for Panchayats) and Article 243ZA (for Municipalities).

subject: Polity
question_text: Article 324 of the Constitution of India provides for the establishment of which one of the following institutions in India? [GEOSCIENTIST 2022]
solution:
(D) The Election Commission of India
Article 324 -329 under Part XV deals with the Election Commission. For further explanation, refer to question 3 and 4 of level I.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements relating to the State Election Commissioner is/are correct? [GEOSCIENTIST 2023]
1. The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Election Commission of India.
2. The State Election Commissioner can be removed only in the same manner as a judge of a High Court.
solution:
(B) 2 only
The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Governor of the state, not by the Election Commission of India as per Article 243K. The State Election Commissioner can be removed only in the same manner and on the same grounds as a Judge of a High Court.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one among the following statements is not correct as per the Constitution of India? [GEOSCIENTIST 2024]

options.a: National Commission for Minorities appoints a Special Officer for linguistic minorities.
options.b: It shall be the duty of the Union to develop the Hindi language as a medium of expression for all the elements of the composite culture of India.
options.c: The word 'Hindustani' is mentioned in the Constitution with reference to the enrichment of the Hindi language.
options.d: Every state shall endeavour to provide adequate facilities for instruction in the mother-tongue at the primary stage of education to children belonging to minority groups.

solution:
(A) National Commission for Minorities appoints a Special Officer for linguistic minorities.
The Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities is appointed by the President, not by the National Commission for Minorities (Article 350B). Article 351: Duty of the Union to promote Hindi, drawing on Hindustani and other languages, Article 350A: States to provide mother-tongue instruction at the primary stage for minority children.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following statements: [GEOSCIENTIST 2024]
1. A special provision was made in the Constitution of India by the Constitution (Eighty-ninth Amendment) Act, 2003 to establish the National Commission for Scheduled Tribes.
2. National Commission for Backward Classes, National Commission for Minorities and National Commission for Scheduled Castes are constitutional bodies.
3. National Commission for Women is not a constitutional body.
Which of the statements given above is/are not correct?
solution:
(C) 2 only
The 89th CA, 2003 inserted Article 338A to establish a separate National Commission for Scheduled Tribes. Out of the three: National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) became a constitutional body through the 102nd Amendment Act, 2018 (Article 338B), National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC) is a constitutional body (Article 338). But the National Commission for Minorities (NCM) is not a constitutional body; it is a statutory body, established under the National Commission for Minorities Act, 1992. The National Commission for Women (NCW) is also a statutory body, not a constitutional one.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following organizations/ bodies in India:
1. The National Commission for Backward Classes
2. The National Human Rights Commission
3. The National Law Commission
4. The National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission
How many of the above are constitutional bodies? [IAS 2023]
solution:
(A) Only one
(1) National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) - Became a constitutional body via the 102nd CA, 2018 (Article 338B). (2) National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) - Statutory Body established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. (3) National Law Commission - Non-constitutional, non-statutory (executive body) set up by the Government of India through executive order. (4) National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC) - Statutory Body established under the Consumer Protection Act, 1986.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following statements about the Backward Class Commission in India:
1. The First Backward Class Commission is popularly known as the Kaka Kalelkar Commission.
2. The Third Backward Class Commission is popularly known as the Mandal Commission.
Which is/are correct? [CDS I 2025]
solution:
(A) 1 only
Only two Backward Class Commissions have been appointed-one in the 1950s under Kaka Kalelkar and the other in the 1970s under B.P. Mandal. The Indra Sawhney case of 1992 is related to the Mandal Commission. The National Commission for Backward Classes was set up in 1993 and was granted constitutional status under Article 338B through the 102nd Constitutional Amendment in 2018.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which statements are correct? [CDS I 2025]
1. The First Law Commission recommended the Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860.
2. The IPC, 1860, has been completely replaced by the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
Code:
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
The Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860, was drafted by the First Law Commission chaired by Thomas Macaulay, set up in 1834 under the Charter Act of 1833. The IPC has been replaced by the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023. Similarly, the CrPC, 1973, and the Indian Evidence Act, 1872, have been replaced by the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023, and the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA), 2023, respectively.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which statements about NITI Aayog are correct? [CDS I 2025]
1. The Prime Minister chairs its Governing Council.
2. The Lieutenant Governor of Ladakh is a member.
Code:
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) was established on January 1, 2015, through an executive resolution of the Government of India, replacing the Planning Commission. The Prime Minister serves as its Chairperson, and the Governing Council includes Chief Ministers of all States, Chief Ministers of Union Territories with legislatures, and Lieutenant Governors of other UTs. Shri Suman K. Bery is the current Vice Chairperson.

subject: Polity
question_text: What is the term of office of the Chairperson and Members of the National Human Rights Commission? [CAPF 2022]

options.a: 5 years or up to 70 years of age
options.b: 3 years or up to 70 years of age
options.c: 5 years or up to 65 years of age
options.d: 3 years or up to 65 years of age

solution:
(B) 3 years or up to 70 years of age
The Chairperson and Members of the NHRC serve for 3 years or until the age of 70, whichever is earlier.

subject: Polity
question_text: The National Human Rights Commission was established under the statute of [CAPF 2023]

options.a: The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
options.b: The Protection and Implementation of Human Rights Act, 1993

solution:
(A) The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is a non-constitutional body of India established under The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. It consists of a Chairman and 5 other members. The chairperson is a former Chief Justice of India or a former Supreme Court Judge. Re-appointment is possible, but further employment under the Central or State government is not permitted after their tenure.

subject: Polity
question_text: Under which one among the following Articles of the Constitution of India, a member of a Civil Service, whether of the Union or of a State, seeks protection from unlawful dismissal from service? [CAPF 2024]
solution:
(B) Article 311
Article 311 of the Constitution of India provides protection to members of the civil services (both Union and State) from arbitrary dismissal, removal, or reduction in rank. It ensures that no civil servant can be dismissed or removed from service except after an inquiry, which follows the principles of natural justice, unless the person is removed due to certain exceptional circumstances.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements with regard to the Anoop Baranwal v. Union of India (2023) case is/are correct?
1. The case was heard by a Constitutional Bench.
2. According to the judgment, the grounds for removal of Election Commissioners shall be the same as the Chief Justice of India.
Select the answer using the code given below: [CAPF 2024]
solution:
(A) 1 only
The Anoop Baranwal v. Union of India (2023) case was indeed heard by a Constitutional Bench of the Supreme Court of India. A Constitutional Bench is typically formed when the case involves substantial questions of law as to the interpretation of the Constitution. According to the judgment in the Anoop Baranwal case, the grounds for the removal of Election Commissioners shall be the same as those for the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC), not the Chief Justice of India (CJI). The Chief Election Commissioner can be removed from office in a manner similar to a judge of the Supreme Court, which requires a resolution passed by Parliament.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements regarding the electoral rolls is/are correct?
1. There is one general electoral roll for every territorial constituency.
2. No person is ineligible for inclusion in the electoral roll on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or any of them.
3. A citizen of India, not less than 18 years of age, can cast his/her vote unless disqualified under a law of an appropriate legislature.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
solution:
(D) 1, 2 and 3
According to Article 325, there shall be one general electoral roll for every territorial constituency for election to either the House of the People or a State Legislative Assembly and also prohibits exclusion from the electoral roll on the basis of religion, race, caste, or sex. As per Article 326, the right to vote is guaranteed to every citizen of India who is 18 years or older, subject to disqualifications under the law (like unsound mind, crime, or corrupt practices).

subject: Polity
question_text: In which of the following years did the East India Company abolish the dyarchy system of Bengal? [SSC CHSL 11/08/2023]
solution:
(A) 1772
In 1772, the East India Company, under Warren Hastings, abolished the dual (dyarchy) system of administration in Bengal, which had been established after the Treaty of Allahabad in 1765. Under the dyarchy system, the Company had the Diwani (revenue collection) rights, while the Nawab retained the Nizamat (administration of justice and law and order). The Treaty of Allahabad was signed in 1765 between: Shah Alam II, the Mughal Emperor, and the British East India Company, represented by Robert Clive after the victory of Britishers in the Battle of Buxar (1764).

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following acts introduced the system of dyarchy in the provincial administration in India? [SSC MTS 30/09/2024]

options.a: The Government of India Act of 1919
options.b: The Government of India Act of 1947
options.c: The Government of India Act of 1935
options.d: The Government of India Act of 1909

solution:
(A) The Government of India Act of 1919
The system of dyarchy in the provinces was introduced by the Government of India Act of 1919. Under this Act, Subjects were divided into "Reserved" (controlled by the Governor and his executive council) and "Transferred" (administered by Indian ministers responsible to the legislative councils).

subject: Polity
question_text: Which among the following Acts established the dual system of control by the British government and the East India Company in India? [SSC MTS 04/05/2023]
solution:
(A) Pitt's India Act, 1784
Under the Pitt's India Act of 1784, a dual system of government consisting of a Board of Governors consisting of 6 members to oversee the administrative functions and a Court of Directors to manage the commercial functions of the Company was introduced. The term 'British possessions in India' was used for the first time in this act. Then Governor General of Bengal - Warren Hastings (1773 - 1785).

subject: Polity
question_text: The proclamation of which Act by the King of England in 1773 paved the way for the establishment of the Supreme Court at Calcutta? [SSC CHSL 03/07/2024]
solution:
(A) Regulating Act
The Regulating Act of 1773 was a significant step by the British Parliament to regulate the affairs of the East India Company and its territories in India. It established a Supreme Court at Calcutta in 1774 under letters patent issued by King George III. Warren Hastings (last Governor of Bengal + first Governor-General of Bengal).

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following was enacted in 1860 in British India? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 13/10/2023]
solution:
(C) Indian Penal Code
The Indian Penal Code (IPC) was enacted in 1860 and came into effect in 1862. It serves as the foundation of criminal law in India, defining various crimes and prescribing punishments. It was drafted under the leadership of Lord Macaulay who was the first law member and the chairman of the first Indian Civil Services Committee in 1854. The Indian Penal Code, 1860 is being replaced by the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, effective from 1st July 2024, as part of broader legal reforms that also replaced the CrPC with Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 and the Indian Evidence Act with Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023. Permanent Settlement of Bengal - Introduced in 1793 by Lord Cornwallis. Hindu Widow's Remarriage Act - Passed in 1856, legalized widow remarriage. Indian Evidence Act - Enacted in 1872, deals with rules of evidence in courts.

subject: Polity
question_text: Under the Regulating Act of 1773, the Supreme Court of Judicature was established by British Emperor in which of the following cities in British India? [SSC JE 06/06/2024]
solution:
(C) Calcutta
Under the Regulating Act of 1773, the Supreme Court of Judicature was established at Fort Williams in Calcutta in 1774. It was the first Supreme Court in British India, set up to administer justice under British law. It was composed of: Chief Justice - Sir Elijah Impey, Puisne (Associate) Justices - (1) Robert Chambers, (2) John Hyde, (3) Samuel Hill.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which act was passed by the British Parliament to control the territories of the East India Company majorly in Bengal in the year 1773? [SSC MTS 20/06/2023]
solution:
(B) Regulating Act
The Regulating Act of 1773 established (1) the Governor-General of Bengal and his Council. (2) Created the Supreme Court at Calcutta. (3) Required Company officials to report back to London, marking the start of formal government control over Company rule. The first Governor general of Bengal was Warren Hastings (1773 - 1785).

subject: Polity
question_text: The East India Company lost all its administrative powers with the enforcement of _______. [SSC MTS 28/10/2024]
solution:
(B) The Government of India Act, 1858
After the Revolt of 1857, also called the 'First War of Independence' by VD Savarkar, the British Crown abolished the East India company and took charge of its administrative functions (also known as Queen's Proclamation or Direct Crown rule). It also abolished the Board of Governors and the Court of Directors. The position of Governor-General of India was upgraded to Viceroy of India, under which Lord Canning became the first Viceroy of India. A new post called the Secretary of State (SOS) was created and he was assisted by a 15-member Council of India based in London to oversee Indian affairs.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Pitts India Act of _______ distinguished between the commercial and political functions of the British East India Company. [SSC CHSL 21/03/2023]
solution:
(C) 1784
The Pitt's India Act of 1784 was passed to address the shortcomings of the Regulating Act of 1773. It distinguished between the commercial and political functions of the British East India Company. The Act also established a Board of Control to oversee political affairs, while the Court of Directors continued to manage commercial activities.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following Acts passed under Warren Hastings was also called 'half-loaf system'? [SSC CGL 19/09/2024]
solution:
(B) Regulating Act 1773
The Regulating Act of 1773 is called the "half-loaf system" because it marked Parliament's first move to curb the East India Company's power without fully subsuming it. The Charter Act of 1813 ended the East India Company's trade monopoly in India, except for trade in tea and with China. It allowed Christian missionaries to operate in India and promoted education by allocating ₹1 lakh annually. The act also asserted the sovereignty of the British Crown over Indian territories. Also, the Charter Act of 1833 is called the Saint Helena Act.

subject: Polity
question_text: As a result of which act did Queen Victoria, who was the monarch of Britain, also became the sovereign of British territories in India, with the title "Empress of India" in 1858? [SSC MTS 10/05/2023]
solution:
(B) Government of India Act
The Government of India Act of 1858 transferred authority from the East India Company to the British Crown. Consequently, Queen Victoria, already monarch of Britain, assumed direct sovereignty over British India. This act laid the groundwork for her later adoption of the title "Empress of India" in 1876. For further explanation, refer to question 8.

subject: Polity
question_text: In 1600, the East India company acquired a charter from _______. [SSC MTS 18/05/2023]
solution:
(A) Queen Elizabeth I
In 1600, the English East India Company received its royal charter from Queen Elizabeth I. The Company's first factory (permanent) was established at Surat in 1612, following the Treaty of Surat with the Mughal Empire. The most recent monarch of the United Kingdom is King Charles III.

subject: Polity
question_text: In which of the following years, were separate electorates introduced in the Imperial Legislative Assembly elections in the name of Morley- Minto Reforms? [SSC CPO 28/06/2024]
solution:
(B) Indian Council Act, 1909
Also known as the Morley-Minto Reforms, it introduced separate electorates for Muslims, marking the start of communal representation in British India. Lord Minto is called the "Father of Communalism" for this policy. Later, the Poona Pact (1932) was signed between Dr. B.R. Ambedkar and Mahatma Gandhi to avoid separate electorates for Dalits (Depressed Classes), as proposed by the British in the Communal Award. Instead, it allowed reserved seats within general electorates. For the first time, an Indian, Satyendra Prasad Sinha, was appointed to the Viceroy's Executive Council as Law Member under this Act.

subject: Polity
question_text: The _______ is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom that partitioned British India into two new independent dominions of India and Pakistan in 1947. [SSC MTS 15/07/2022]
solution:
(C) Indian Independence Act
The Indian Independence Act of 1947 was passed by the British Parliament to partition British India into two independent dominions, India and Pakistan, on August 15, 1947. This Act marked the end of British rule in India. The last Viceroy of India was Lord Louis Mountbatten, who oversaw the partition and transfer of power. C. Rajagopalachari was the first and last Indian to serve as the Governor-General of India from 1948 to 1950.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Charter Act of 1813 extended the East India Company's rule in India to another _______ years. [SSC MTS 11/05/2023]
solution:
(C) 20
The Charter Act of 1813 extended the East India Company's rule for another 20 years (until 1833). It ended the Company's trade monopoly, except for trade in tea and trade with China. It also allowed Christian missionaries to come to India and a provision that authorized local governments to impose taxes.

subject: Polity
question_text: In India which Act divided the powers between the Central and units in terms of three lists? [SSC CHSL 09/08/2023]
solution:
(D) 1935
The Government of India Act, 1935 introduced a division of powers between the Central and provincial governments through the three lists: Union List (Central subjects), Provincial List (State subjects), Concurrent List (Subjects shared by both Central and Provincial governments) marking the evolution of federal governance in India.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which act of the British Parliament was also known as East India Company Act 1793? [SSC MTS 19/05/2023]
solution:
(B) The Charter Act, 1793
The Charter Act of 1793 is also known as the East India Company Act of 1793. It renewed the East India Company's charter for another 20 years and outlined the administration of the company's territories. The Act also granted the company certain privileges and powers, including the right to trade and maintain an army in India.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which among the following Acts discarded the 'dyarchy' system at the provincial level and allowed for the emergence of popularly elected provincial legislatures? [SSC MTS 09/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Government of India Act 1935
The Government of India Act, 1935 discarded the dyarchy system at the provincial level and allowed for fully elected provincial legislatures. It also introduced dyarchy at the Centre, where the government was divided into areas of provincial autonomy and central control. The Act also established a Federal Court to settle disputes between the central and provincial governments.

subject: Polity
question_text: Under which of the following Acts, the Board of Control was established in England to control and supervise the administration of British India? [SSC CGL 18/09/2024]
solution:
(D) Pitt's India Act of 1784
The Pitt's India Act of 1784 established the Board of Control in England to supervise and control the administration of British India. For further explanation, refer to question 9.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following Acts, enacted by the British Parliament, provided for the establishment of a Law Commission for the consolidation and codification of Indian Laws? [SSC CPO 03/10/2023]
solution:
(A) Charter Act, 1833
This act is also known as Saint Helena Act 1833. The Law Commission was organized under Lord Macaulay to codify all Indian laws. Under this Act, the Governor-General of Bengal became the Governor-General of India. Lord William Bentick became the first Governor-General of India. Bombay and Madras were deprived of their power to legislate. Also the Act led to a complete abolition of monopoly even in terms of tea and china trade.

subject: Polity
question_text: How many members (elected and nominated) were there in the Council of State under the Government of India Act 1919 passed by the Parliament of the United Kingdom to expand participation of Indians in the government of India? [SSC CPO 29/06/2024]
solution:
(A) 60
The GOI Act 1919 was based on the recommendations of Edwin Montagu, the Secretary of State for India and Lord Chelmsford, the Viceroy of India. So, GOI Act 1919 also known as Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms. It introduced dyarchy in provinces with divided subjects (Transferred to Indian ministers, Reserved under British control) and established two houses at the Centre : Council of State (Upper House) and Legislative Assembly (Lower House). It expanded the provision for separate electorates for Sikhs, and Anglo-Indians. The Council of State had 60 members.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which Act passed by British Government of India abolished the Council of India? [SSC MTS 14/07/2022]
solution:
(C) Government of India Act of 1935
It abolished the Council of India, established by the Government of India Act 1858. It abolished dyarchy in the provinces and introduced Provincial autonomy. It provided for the establishment of the All India Federation which consists of princely states and provinces as units. Although the Federation never came into existence because the princely states did not join it.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following was NOT included in the Pitt's India Act of 1784? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 10/12/2024]

options.a: All the debts of the East India Company were taken over by the British Government
options.b: The governance of India was handed over to the Governor General and a council consisting of three members
options.c: A committee of six government appointees, known as the Board of Control, was formed
options.d: Madras and Bombay presidencies were brought under Bengal presidency

solution:
(D) Madras and Bombay presidencies were brought under Bengal presidency.
It was the Regulating Act of 1773 that brought the Madras and Bombay presidencies under the control of the Bengal presidency in terms of administrative oversight. The Pitt's India Act of 1784 took over the debts of the East India Company by the British government and established a Board of Control with six members to oversee the company's administration. It also transferred the governance of India to the Governor-General and a council of three members.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following Acts introduced 'provincial autonomy' by discontinuing the application of dyarchy? [SSC CGL 18/09/2024]
solution:
(D) Government of India Act, 1935
The Government of India Act, 1935 introduced provincial autonomy by discontinuing the dyarchy system that was introduced by the Government of India Act, 1919 at the provincial level and instead introduced dyarchy at the centre.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following Acts provided the title of the Viceroy to the Governor General of India? [SSC PHASE XII 24/06/2024]
solution:
(B) Government of India Act, 1858
The Government of India Act, 1858 provided the title of Viceroy to the Governor-General of India. It marked the end of the rule of the East India Company and the beginning of direct control by the British Crown over India (Queen's proclamation). The Viceroy was the representative of the British Crown in India and had extensive administrative powers. The Act also provided for the post of Secretary of State, a British MP to take reports from the viceroy and submit in the British Parliament. This change was a result of the Indian Rebellion of 1857, which led to significant reorganization of the administrative structure in India.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following statement about the Government of India Act, 1935. [SSC MTS 04/09/2023]
1. This act was enacted by the British Parliament.
2. This act was divided into two separate acts for the India and Burma Government.
3. This act commenced from 1 April 1935.
Select the correct statement(s) using the code given below.
solution:
(D) Both 1 and 3
The Government of India Act, 1935 was indeed enacted by the British Parliament. While the Act did provide for the separation of India and Burma's governance, Burma became a separate colony with its own government after the Government of India Act, 1935, but the Act was not technically "divided" into two separate acts. Burma was separated in 1937 through a separate Burma Act. The Act commenced on 1 April 1935 introducing the concept of a federal structure for India.

subject: Polity
question_text: Under which Act was the rule of British Crown established over India? [SSC MTS 14/09/2023]
solution:
(D) The government of India Act, 1858
For explanation, refer to question 8 and 25.

subject: Polity
question_text: Who propounded the concept of 'Rule of Law'? [SSC MTS 11/09/2023]
solution:
(A) AV Dicey
The concept of 'Rule of Law' was propounded by A.V. Dicey, a British jurist, in his book "Introduction to the Study of the Law of the Constitution". India has borrowed the concept of Rule of Law from Britain.

subject: Polity
question_text:
With reference to Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909, consider the following statements.
a) They are also called the Indian Councils Act, 1909.
b) They increased the strength of Legislative Councils.
c) The right to separate electorate was given to the Muslims.
Which of the above statements is/are correct? [SSC CGL 11/09/2024]
solution:
(C) a, b, c
The Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909, also known as the Indian Councils Act, 1909. The Act increased the strength of the Legislative Councils at both the central and provincial levels. It granted separate electorates to Muslims, allowing them to elect their own representatives to the legislative bodies. Additionally, it allowed members of the councils to move resolutions and discuss the budget.

subject: Polity
question_text: Under which of the following Acts of British India did the British government take direct control of the administration of India in its own hands? [SSC CPO 28/06/2024]
solution:
(A) Government of India Act 1858
The Government of India Act, 1858 marked the end of the East India Company's rule and transferred the administration of India directly to the British Crown. This Act was passed after the Revolt of 1857 and led to the establishment of the Viceroy of India, with Lord Canning becoming the first Viceroy.

subject: Polity
question_text: Under which Act did the individual members of the Executive Council have their own portfolio? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 10/12/2024]
solution:
(C) The Indian Councils Act of 1861
The Indian Councils Act of 1861 introduced the portfolio system, under which individual members of the Executive Council were given charge of specific departments (portfolios). Then Viceroy - Lord Canning.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Consider the following statements: [IB ACIO 17/01/2024]
1. The Government of India Act of 1858 was a response to the Indian Rebellion of 1857 and the East India Company lost all its administrative powers following the Legislative Act of 1858, and its Indian possessions and armed forces were taken over by the Crown.
2. Indian Council Act (Morley-Minto Act) of 1907 introduced separate electorates for Muslims in India.
3. The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms of 1919 initiated the Diarchy system in the provincial governments.
4. The Government of India Act of 1935 provided for the establishment of an All-India Federation and introduced provincial autonomy.
Which of the above statements are correct?
solution:
(C) 1, 3 and 4
The Morley-Minto Reforms, also known as the Indian Councils Act, of 1909, not 1907, introduced separate electorates for Muslims. Hence, statement 2 is incorrect.

subject: Polity
question_text: When did the new judicial system of setting up two courts (Criminal and civil) in each district start? [SSC CPO 05/06/2024]
solution:
(B) 1772 C.E.
The new judicial system of setting up two courts (Civil and Criminal) in each district was introduced in 1772 by Warren Hastings, the first Governor-General of Bengal. This was part of the Judicial Plan of 1772, where Diwani Adalat (civil court) and Faujdari Adalat (criminal court) were established in each district.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which of the following Acts provided for a system of tribunals and a ban on strikes? [SSC MTS 23/10/2024]
solution:
(B) The Trade Disputes Act, 1929
The Trade Disputes Act, 1929 was passed to control growing labor unrest and strikes. It set up tribunals to settle industrial disputes, banned strikes and lockouts in public utility services without a prior notice of at least 14 days. Viceroy at that time: Lord Irwin (1926 - 1931).

subject: Polity
question_text: In the year 1946, a three-tier confederation for transfer of power to India was proposed by the _______. [SSC MTS 09/10/2024]
solution:
(C) Cabinet Mission
The Cabinet Mission was sent by the British Government to India in March 1946 to discuss and plan for the transfer of power from British rule to Indian leadership. Members - (1) Lord Pethick-Lawrence - Secretary of State for India (Head of the Mission), (2) Sir Stafford Cripps - President of the Board of Trade, (3) A.V. Alexander - First Lord of the Admiralty. It proposed a three-tier confederation for the transfer of power to India. It aimed to keep India united while giving autonomy to provinces and groups of provinces. The plan included: (1) A Union government handling defense, foreign affairs, and communications. (2) Groups of provinces with their own constitutions. (3) Individual provinces with autonomy over all other subjects.

subject: Polity
question_text: Warren Hastings became the Governor-General of Bengal from Governor of Bengal after the passing of which of the following Acts? [SSC CHSL 09/07/2024]
solution:
(B) Regulating Act, 1773
The Regulating Act of 1773 made Warren Hastings the first Governor-General of Bengal, upgrading his earlier position as Governor of Bengal. The Indian Councils Act of 1861, passed under Lord Canning, allowed Indians to be nominated as non-official members in the Viceroy's council. It restored legislative powers to Bombay and Madras and introduced the portfolio system.

subject: Polity
question_text: The provision for separate electorate for Muslims was given in [CDS I 2022]
solution:
(A) Morley- Minto Reforms, 1909
The Morley-Minto Reforms (Indian Councils Act, 1909) introduced the system of separate electorates for Muslims, allowing them to elect their own representatives. For this, Minto is called the 'Father of Communalism'. For further explanation, refer to question 13.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following is not correct about the Indian Independence Act, 1947? [CDS I 2023]

options.a: The Bill for the Indian Independence Act was introduced in Parliament on July 4 and received the Royal Assent on July 18, 1947.
options.b: Act of 1947 laid down the basic framework of the Indian Constitution.
options.c: The Act provided for the establishment of two independent Dominions to be known as India and Pakistan.
options.d: The Act empowered the Constituent Assembly of each Dominion to have unlimited power to frame and adopt any constitution.

solution:
(B) Act of 1947 laid down the basic framework of the Indian Constitution.
The Indian Independence Act, 1947 did not lay down the framework of the Indian Constitution. Instead, it provided for two independent dominions: India and Pakistan empowering each Constituent Assembly to frame its own constitution. It ended British suzerainty over Indian states and came into effect on 15th August 1947. Lord Mountbatten was the last British Viceroy of India and also became the first Governor-General of independent India.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements about the Indian Councils Act of 1861 is/are correct?
1. It enlarged the Governor General's Council for the purpose of making laws.
2. The Governor General was not authorised to increase the number of members.
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [NDA II 2021]
solution:
(A) 1 only
The Indian Councils Act of 1861 marked the beginning of Indian representation in legislation as it enlarged the Governor-General's Council by including Indians like Raja of Benaras and Sir Dinkar Rao. Also, the Governor-General was empowered to increase the number of members, meaning he had the authority to increase the number. Hence, statement 2 is incorrect. The Act also introduced the portfolio system and restored the legislative powers of the Bombay and Madras presidencies.

subject: Polity
question_text: The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms led to the enactment of which one among the following landmark Acts? [CBI 2023]
solution:
(B) The Government of India Act, 1919
The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms led to the Government of India Act, 1919, which introduced Dyarchy in provinces-dividing subjects into reserved (e.g., police, finance) handled by British control and transferred (e.g., education, health) handled by Indian ministers. It established a bicameral legislature at the Centre with the Council of State (60 members) and Legislative Assembly (145 members). The Act expanded separate electorates for Muslims, Sikhs, Indian Christians, and Anglo-Indians. For further explanation, refer to question 21.

subject: Polity
question_text:
Which of the following statements about the Mountbatten Plan is/are correct?
1. The elections to the Constituent Assembly were held in 1946 under the Mountbatten Plan.
2. The Muslim League's demand for a separate Constituent Assembly was conceded in the Mountbatten Plan.
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [CBI 2023]
solution:
(B) 2 only
Elections to the Constituent Assembly were held in 1946, but not under the Mountbatten Plan-they were held under the Cabinet Mission Plan. The Mountbatten Plan (June 3, 1947) accepted the Muslim League's demand for a separate Constituent Assembly for Pakistan, leading to the partition of India.

subject: Polity
question_text: 'Provincial Autonomy' was the most redeeming feature of which one of the following Acts? [CISF AC 2022]
solution:
(B) The Government of India Act, 1935
The Government of India Act, 1935 introduced provincial autonomy by discontinuing the dyarchy system that was introduced by the Government of India Act, 1919 at the provincial level and introduced dyarchy at the centre.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which one of the following Acts added a Law member to Governor General's Council in India? [CISF AC 2022]
solution:
(C) The Charter Act of 1833
The Charter Act of 1833 added a Law Member (first was Thomas Babington Macaulay) to the Governor-General's Council for legislative purposes. Macaulay also introduced the famous Macaulay's Minutes on Education (1835), promoting English education in India. He advocated the Downward Filtration Theory, which aimed to educate a small class of Indians in English who would then educate the masses.

subject: Polity
question_text:
In the Government of India Act 1919, the functions of Provincial Government were divided into "Reserved" and "Transferred" subjects. Which of the following were treated as "Reserved" subjects?
1. Administration of Justice
2. Local Self-Government
3. Land Revenue
4. Police
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [IAS 2022]
solution:
(C) 1, 3 and 4
Under the Government of India Act, 1919, subjects were divided as follows: (1) Reserved Subjects (under the Governor and Executive Council): Administration of Justice, Land Revenue, Police. (2) Transferred Subjects (under Indian Ministers): Local Self-Government, Public Health, Education. This division of powers introduced the dyarchy system at the provincial level.

subject: Polity
question_text: By which one of the following Acts was the Governor General of Bengal designated as the Governor General of India? [IAS 2023]
solution:
(D) The Charter Act of 1833
In the Charter Act of 1833, the Governor General of Bengal was designated as the Governor General of India. So, Lord William Bentick (1833 - 1835) became the first Governor-General of India.

subject: Polity
question_text:
With reference to the Government of India Act, 1935, consider the following statements:
1. It provided for the establishment of an All India Federation based on the union of the British Indian Provinces and Princely States.
2. Defence and Foreign Affairs were kept under the control of the federal legislature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct? [IAS 2024]
solution:
(A) 1 only
The Government of India Act, 1935 proposed an All India Federation consisting of British Indian provinces and Princely States (though the federation never fully materialized as princely states did not join). However, Defence and Foreign Affairs were "reserved subjects" under the Government of India Act, 1935. These remained under the exclusive control of the Governor General (representing the British Crown) and not under the federal legislature. So, while the Act created a federal structure in theory, real power like defence stayed with the British.

subject: Polity
question_text: Which among the following Acts provided for the establishment of a Supreme Court of Justice at Calcutta for Europeans, their employees and the citizens of India? [NDA I 2025]
solution:
(A) The Regulating Act of 1773
The Regulating Act of 1773 made the Governor of Bengal the Governor-General of Bengal, with Warren Hastings as the first to hold the post. It also established the Supreme Court at Fort William, Calcutta. The Charter Act of 1833 changed the title to Governor-General of India. Later, the Government of India Act of 1858 abolished this post, introducing the Viceroy of India and the Secretary of State for India.

subject: Polity
question_text: In British India, which one among the following Acts permitted detention without trial for up to two years? [NDA I 2025]
solution:
(B) Rowlatt Act, 1919
The Rowlatt Act of 1919, officially known as the Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act, replaced the temporary Defence of India Act of 1915. Based on the recommendations of Justice Sidney Rowlatt, this "Black Act" - a term notably used by Mahatma Gandhi to highlight its unjust nature - permitted detention without trial for up to two years. Widespread opposition to this act directly contributed to protests that culminated in events such as the Jallianwala Bagh massacre.
